title: Stephen Foster and That Old, Weird America
author: https://hedgehogreview.com/contributors/ed-simon
text:
Thomas Jefferson died a few minutes after noon on the fiftieth anniversary of the signing of his Declaration of Independence. Attended by a University of Virginia medical professor, along with a grandson and a grandson-in-law, the third president reportedly asked whether it was the Fourth of July, before answering himself, “It soon will be,” and then quite promptly dying. Six hours after Jefferson’s death, John Adams—the man whom Jefferson beat in the 1800 presidential election—suffered a heart attack at Peacefield, his “Old House” in Quincy, Massachusetts. As is well known, Adams’s last words were the erroneous “Thomas Jefferson still survives.” (Whether this was grudging or reassuring is uncertain.) 

That these two architects of the Republic, the last and most significant pair of the revolutionary generation, died on the half-centennial appeared providential. The then-occupant of the White House, John Quincy Adams (son of the second president), saw the coincidental deaths as “visible and palpable remarks of Divine Favor.” 

Another palpable remark of divine favor happened on that Independence Day as well, in a place far south of Peacefield and north of Monticello, though farther west than either. In the burgeoning frontier metropolis of Pittsburgh, Stephen C. Foster, the most significant author of parlor and minstrel songs in nineteenth-century America, was born. “The North and the South Poles of the American Revolution,” Steve Erickson notes in his contribution to A New Literary History of America, “never knew Stephen Foster.… But Foster’s melody already waits in the American night.” Neither a philosopher nor a legislator, a gentleman-farmer or a statesman, Foster was rather the first of an American type: the pop star. 

Foster would become the celebrated author of hundreds of songs, many of which, including “Oh Susanna!,” “Camptown Races,” “Jeannie with the Light Brown Hair,” and “Beautiful Dreamer.” have passed into the semi-eternal firmament to become folk tunes rather than mere compositions by a distinct author. These are works that all can recognize, even if none of us listen to them, enduring missives from what critic Greil Marcus described in Invisible Republic: Bob Dylan’s Basement Tapes as that “old, weird America.” Adams, the Northern revolutionary elitist and Jefferson the Southern slave-owning Republican, were both still attached to the old world and its ways. By contrast, Foster was neither a Northerner nor a Southerner but a Westerner, which made all the difference. 

The Founders conceived of a particular nation, an Enlightenment experiment firmly European, but the popularity of Foster signaled the emergence of a very different country, a nation of self-mythologizing grifters and confidence men, of boot-strappers and self-inventors. Allegorically, Foster—in both his subjects and his example—was the first of a new type of man: the American.

Among the pantheon of popular American composers, Foster stands with Lead Belly, Woody Guthrie, Pete Seeger, and Bob Dylan as one of the few who have become synonymous with our national music. Coming out of the same Romantic tradition that formed Edgar Allan Poe and Nathaniel Hawthorne, Foster’s was a sentimental vision, an eerie one characterized by the gossamer uncanniness of its description of “Starlight and dewdrops are waiting for thee… Lull’d by the moonlight have all pass’d away.” 

Dead almost three decades before Thomas Edison cut the first wax cylinder, the free-spending Foster, through the selling of sheet music alone, became the most popular songwriter on both sides of the Mason-Dixon Line and the Mississippi River. In the twentieth century, his melodies—which can be both schmaltzy and aching, kitschy and beautiful—would be quoted as musical motifs by Aaron Copland, Charles Ives, and Steve Reich. And long after his death, his songs would be covered by everyone from Willie Nelson to Yo-Yo Ma.

“I go back to Stephen Foster,” said Dylan in a 2003 Los Angeles Times interview, eventually dedicating a whole chapter to the 1849 “Nelly Was a Lady” in The Philosophy of Modern Song. He could not help marveling at the prodigious talents of his prolific predecessor. “A lot of sad songs have been written,” Dylan writes, “but none sadder than this.” 

Glossing Greil Marcus’s argument about that old, weird America in New York Magazine, Luc (now Lucy) Sante described how there is a national idiom that is the “playground of God, Satan, tricksters, Puritans, confidence men, illuminati, braggarts, preachers, anonymous poets of all stripes.” That playground is very much the cosmos of Foster, one antithetical to the classical traditionalism of an Adams or the rectilinear coolness of a Jefferson. That undiscovered country of the old, weird America can be detected in the 1852 tune, “Maggie by My Side,” a sea shanty celebrating the marriage of a boat captain and his faithful dog. Or consider the steamboat race in “The Glendy Burk” from the 1860s, with its frenetic, vaguely ominous, herky-jerky rhythm. Even the less obscure songs can reflect a distinctly macabre sensibility, as in the 1855 number in which the narrator matter of factly sings “Some folks like to sigh… Some folks long to die,” or the Dada-esque line from 1848’s “Oh! Susanna!”: “The sun so hot I froze to death.” Such surreal American tableaux would have been foreign or distasteful to Jefferson or Adams but familiar to those citizens of the invisible republic that includes the cast of all-American characters in The Band’s 1968 “The Weight.” with “Carmen and the devil walking side by side,” or novelist George Saunders’s 2017 Lincoln in the Bardo, set in the liminal gloaming in which “Everything was real; inconceivably real, infinitely dear.” 

Dylan’s mercurial transformations are even more evident in Foster, though in a different way. If Dylan is a cipher, Foster is a lacuna. His is music authored not by a man but by that other songwriter named “Traditional.” Of course, he did come from a place, and a privileged one at that. Stephen’s father, the Virginian William Barclay Foster, was a three-time state legislator and former mayor of Allegheny, Pennsylvania (now the Northside of Pittsburgh). By 1826, the elder Foster had built a handsome white manor with green-trim shutters on what-was-then the Philadelphia Turnpike in the newly founded community of Lawrenceville. On that fateful Independence Day, William gathered notables and worthies in his garden, including the mayor of Pittsburgh, to toast the fiftieth anniversary of the United States, cheers and fireworks mingling with the pained cries of Eliza Clayland Tomlinson Foster delivering a son named Stephen on the second floor of the house. From the beginning, there have been those “visible and palpable remarks of Divine Favor” in the birthday of the most American of composers being shared both with the United States and the day on which two of its founders died, as if they handed America off to him. This was a songwriter who, as Christopher Lynch writes in Formulating Foster: Stephen C. Foster and the Creation of a National Musical Myth, supposedly held attitudes that were “representative of American democracy,” at least in the minds of later scholars who burnished the hagiography by highlighting that July Fourth birthday. 

Lawrenceville, with its rows of handsome townhomes that wind along narrow streets up the side of the hill from the Allegheny River, is uncharacteristically older than many of the surrounding communities in Pittsburgh, with a feeling akin to Baltimore, Boston, and Brooklyn as much as anything in the Rust Belt. But there are few traces of Foster in his birthplace, all the better to reinforce the myth that he was a songwriter who came from nowhere and everywhere. A decade after the white-cottage-with-green-trim-windows was built, the bank would repossess the handsome home that Foster’s father built and replace it with a mansard-roofed Second Empire house on a small parcel of land in the now-dense urban neighborhood. At the University of Pittsburgh, a well-appointed one-room museum dedicated to the songwriter’s life is maintained by the Center for American Music. And across the street there used to sit a statue by the Italian sculptor Giuseppe Moretti, unveiled in 1900 and (appropriately) removed in 2018 after the unanimous decision of Pittsburgh’s Art Commission objecting to it its representation of “Old Ned”—a smiling, shoeless enslaved man strumming a banjo at Foster’s feet as the composer acts as amanuensis for raw, untutored, and primitive inspiration.

And therein lies the crux of Foster’s troubling legacy. Among the genres for which he composed and arranged (parlor tunes and minstrel songs), it was the minstrel songs on which his fame was dependent. With lyrics written in a pantomime of black dialect to be sung by white performers who darkened their faces with burnt cork, the minstrel songs are an uncomfortable reminder of what literary scholar Eric Lott described as “love and theft,” that is, a predatory obsession with blackness and the appropriation of an imagined blackness. Describing how minstrel performance encompassed “folklore, dance, jokes, songs, instrumental tunes, skits, mock oratory, satire, and racial and gender cross-dressing or impersonation,” Lott, in Love and Theft: Blackface Minstrelsy and the American Working Class, concludes that this first native performance genre depended on a kind of white supremacist dialectic. According to Lott, the minstrel show was “Underwritten by envy as well as repulsion, sympathetic identification as well as fear,” so that even in mockery white performers imitated their own coveted version of blackness while bolstering structural violence against actual black people. 

Pennsylvania’s first abolition act was passed in 1780; at the time of Foster’s birth, blacks made up only about three percent of Pittsburgh’s population, mostly focused in the neighborhood that was then called “Hayti” and that today is the still largely African-American Hill District. Most of Foster’s experience with actual black communities was theoretical, but as if to confirm Lott’s thesis, the aspiring young composer had announced his desire to become the “best Ethiopian songwriter.” Long before Eminem or Elvis, Foster was playacting his own version of blackness (an American tradition that he inaugurated). That many of his melodies are Scots-Irish ballads, with occasional German elements, is beside the point, because verisimilitude was not Foster’s concern. While he occasionally incorporated elements of true black spirituals, perhaps learned from his nanny, the music itself was always more fantastical (and offensive) than truthful. These are songs in which the “head must bow, and the back will have to bend, / Wherever the darkey may go,” as he writes in “My Old Kentucky Home,” which remains the state song of that commonwealth. “Gone from the earth to a better land I know, / I hear the gentle voices calling ‘Old Black Joe’” sings the narrator in that eponymous song. “Sadly I roam,” begins the narrator of one of his most popular songs, “Still longing for de old plantation, / And for de old folks at home.”  

The orator and abolitionist Frederick Douglass called Foster’s tunes “heart songs,” claiming in an 1855 essay that they awaken “sympathies for the slave, in which anti-slavery principles take root, grow, and flourish.” The songwriter himself was largely apolitical, though of course that is its own type of politics. Like many white Americans, especially in his century, Foster may have loved blackness but had problems with black people. That Douglass would enlist America’s most popular songwriter in his own cause makes strategic sense, however true it was or even whether Douglass himself fully believed it. A savvy, if unsuccessful, businessman, Foster did not want to alienate Southern fans, and indeed many of his admirers assumed he was a son of antebellum Dixie. To listen to “Old Folks at Home” or “Massa’s in De Cold Ground” is to hear an author apparently more at home with magnolia-scented plantations and Spanish-moss-covered town squares rather than with the steel furnaces and soot-belching factories of a Northern industrial city. Yet the entirety of Foster’s Southern exposure consisted of a single honeymoon riverboat trip down the Mississippi. Which may partly explain, though hardly forgive, such lyrics as “Massa make de darkeys love him, / Cayse he was so kind.” Obscuring and even revising the brutality of chattel slavery, such lyrics served as a poetry of flattery for the South and moral placation for the North. 

Ironically, Foster himself did not particularly care for the minstrel songs, which he considered trashy and vulgar. He preferred the parlor tunes, not least for their debts to European symphonic music. Part of the difficulty for contemporary listeners is that regardless of the lyrics (heart songs or not), the melodies of the minstrel tunes are so often beautiful. “Foster’s songs lived on because they served a vital function in their society,” writes JoAnne O’Connell in The Life and Songs of Stephen Foster, “but they would not have survived had his genius for melody not kept people singing them over and over again.” Just listen to “Hard Times Come Again No More,” written in 1854 and popular among both Union and Confederate soldiers: “There’s a pale drooping maiden who toils her life away / With a worn heart whose better days are o’er: / Though her voice would be merry, ’tis sighing all the day.… Hard Times, Hard Times, come again no more.” Obviously, songs like that will be continue to be covered as long as people have need of consolation, and indeed there is a distinctly Celtic version, with a mournful closing pipe solo, produced by the Chieftains in 2012, a twangy version by Emmylou Harris from 1992, a soulful rendering by Mavis Staples in 2004, and a powerful rendition by Bruce Springsteen in 2009 in which Clarence Clemons’s plaintive saxophone sounds like a plea. The writer knew hard times, after all. Living beyond one’s means was a family trait, and Foster would die broke in New York’s Bellevue Hospital during the last year of the Civil War. Running a high fever and possibly suffering from alcohol withdrawal, he took his final leave in 1864, the same year that Battle of the Wilderness and the March to the Sea consumed even more young American lives. He had only 37 cents in his pocket (one less than his age) and an envelope on which he had scratched some half-formed lyrics. “Dear friends and gentle hearts,” it begins, but death cut short whatever was to follow.
